title: Visa options for doctors moving to Australia
metaTitle: Visa Options for IMG Doctors in Australia
metaDescription: A general overview of Australian visa options for IMGs, subclass 482, 186, 189/190 and DAMA. General information only, not migration advice.
summaryMarkdown:
If you're an international medical graduate planning to practise in Australia, you'll usually need both **a visa** that lets you live and work here **and** registration with the Medical Board through AHPRA. These are two separate processes run by two different bodies, and meeting one does not satisfy the other. [[cite:registration-and-visa-separate]]

This page is a **general overview only**. Migration is tightly regulated, circumstances vary, and rules change. It is not migration advice. For your situation, use a **registered migration agent** regulated by MARA, or refer to the Department of Home Affairs. [[cite:use-registered-migration-agent]]

Most doctors enter on an **employer-sponsored** visa. 'Medical Practitioner' occupations appear on the relevant occupation lists, with the Medical Board of Australia named as the assessing authority. [[cite:medical-practitioner-on-lists]]

flow.label: Registration and visa are separate
flow.note: Your AHPRA registration and your visa are two different processes.

flow.label: Secure a sponsored job
flow.note: An approved employer offers the role and nominates it.

flow.label: Choose the visa stream
flow.note: Skills in Demand (subclass 482): Specialist Skills, Core Skills or Labour Agreement.

flow.label: Apply and meet requirements
flow.note: Skills assessment, visa English, health and character checks.

flow.label: Towards permanent residence
flow.note: Subclass 186, or a points-tested 189 or 190, can lead to PR.

sections.heading: A visa and AHPRA registration are separate
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Two different things:

- A **visa** (from the Department of Home Affairs) gives you the legal right to enter, live and work in Australia.
- **Registration** with the Medical Board (via AHPRA) gives you the legal right to *practise medicine*. [[cite:registration-and-visa-separate]]

You generally need both. The visa English standard is also **separate** from the AHPRA registration English standard, for example, the subclass 482 requires a minimum such as IELTS 5.0 overall and in each component, or equivalent, unless exempt. [[cite:visa-482-english]] AHPRA's registration English requirements are typically higher (see [English requirements](/english)). Check both against their official sources.

sections.heading: Skills in Demand visa (subclass 482)
sections.bodyMarkdown:
The Skills in Demand (SID) visa lets an approved employer sponsor a skilled worker for a position they can't fill locally. It replaced the Temporary Skill Shortage (TSS) visa for nominations from 7 December 2024. [[cite:sid-replaced-tss]] It has three streams: [[cite:sid-three-streams]]

- **Core Skills**, occupation on the Core Skills Occupation List (CSOL); pay at least the Core Skills Income Threshold (CSIT); ≥1 year experience; skills assessment if required; English unless exempt; stay up to 4 years (5 for Hong Kong passport holders). [[cite:sid-core-skills]] The CSIT is **AUD 76,515** for nominations lodged 1 July 2025-30 June 2026. [[cite:csit-amount]]
- **Specialist Skills**, occupation in ANZSCO Major Groups 1, 2, 4, 5 or 6; salary meeting the higher Specialist Skills Income Threshold (SSIT); up to 4 years (5 for HK). [[cite:sid-specialist-skills]] The SSIT is **AUD 141,210** for the same period. [[cite:ssit-amount]]
- **Labour Agreement**, occupation specified under a labour agreement (which can include a DAMA); generally ≥12 months experience; up to 4 years (5 for HK). [[cite:sid-labour-agreement]]

Income thresholds are indexed annually and change from 1 July, always confirm the current figure. [[cite:thresholds-indexed]]

sections.heading: Permanent employer-sponsored: subclass 186
sections.bodyMarkdown:
The Employer Nomination Scheme (subclass 186) is a **permanent** visa for skilled workers nominated by an employer, with Direct Entry, Temporary Residence Transition and Labour Agreement streams. [[cite:ens-186-three-streams]]

Under **Direct Entry**, you generally must be under 45 when you apply (unless exempt), be nominated for a CSOL occupation, have ≥3 years relevant experience (unless exempt), provide a positive skills assessment (unless exempt), and meet English, health and character requirements. [[cite:ens-186-direct-entry]] Doctors who start on a 482 may have a pathway to permanent residence through this scheme, depending on circumstances, a registered migration agent can advise. [[cite:sid-to-pr-pathway]]

sections.heading: Points-tested skilled visas (subclass 189 and 190)
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Some doctors qualify for a **points-tested, not employer-sponsored** permanent visa. You submit an Expression of Interest and must be **invited** to apply.

- **Skilled Independent (subclass 189)**, invited skilled workers, permanent, anywhere in Australia; needs a listed occupation, skills assessment, invitation and a passing points test. [[cite:visa-189]]
- **Skilled Nominated (subclass 190)**, similar but requires nomination by a state or territory; also permanent. [[cite:visa-190]]

Meeting the points threshold does **not** guarantee an invitation; these pathways can be competitive and state-dependent.

sections.heading: Regional options: DAMA
sections.bodyMarkdown: A **Designated Area Migration Agreement (DAMA)** is a formal agreement between the Australian Government and a state/territory or regional authority that provides access to more occupations than the standard program. [[cite:dama-what]] It operates as a two-tier framework, a five-year head agreement under which employers request labour agreements with agreed occupations and concessions, using the subclass 482, 494 and 186 programs. [[cite:dama-how]] For doctors heading to regional or rural areas, a DAMA may open occupations or concessions not otherwise available; arrangements are region-specific.

sections.heading: Get regulated migration advice
sections.bodyMarkdown:
Choosing and applying for a visa is a regulated activity with real consequences if it goes wrong. This page can't tell you which visa is right for you.

- Use a **registered migration agent** regulated by MARA, which maintains the register and oversees the profession. [[cite:mara-role]]
- Before paying anyone for help, the Department of Home Affairs advises checking who can lawfully help with a visa application. [[cite:home-affairs-who-can-help]]
- Always confirm current rules, thresholds and occupation lists directly on the official Home Affairs website.

Nothing here is migration advice, treat it as a starting map, then speak to a registered MARA agent about your own situation.

claims.text: A visa to live and work in Australia is granted by Home Affairs, while the right to practise medicine comes from separate registration with the Medical Board via AHPRA.
Migration advice should come from a registered migration agent regulated by MARA, or the official Department of Home Affairs website.
Medical practitioner occupations appear on the occupation lists used for the subclass 186 and 482 visas, with the Medical Board of Australia as the assessing authority.
Subclass 482 applicants must meet a minimum English standard, such as IELTS 5.0 overall and in each component or equivalent, unless exempt.
The Skills in Demand visa (subclass 482) replaced the Temporary Skill Shortage visa, applying to nominations from 7 December 2024.
The subclass 482 has three streams: Core Skills, Specialist Skills and Labour Agreement.
The 482 Core Skills stream needs a CSOL occupation, CSIT/AMSR salary, ≥1 year experience, skills assessment if required, and English, with stay up to 4 years (5 for Hong Kong holders).
The Core Skills Income Threshold is AUD 76,515 for nominations lodged 1 July 2025 to 30 June 2026.
The 482 Specialist Skills stream needs an occupation in ANZSCO Major Groups 1, 2, 4, 5 or 6 with a salary meeting the SSIT, with stay up to 4 years (5 for Hong Kong holders).
The Specialist Skills Income Threshold is AUD 141,210 for nominations lodged 1 July 2025 to 30 June 2026.
The 482 Labour Agreement stream needs an occupation specified under a labour agreement and generally ≥12 months experience, with stay up to 4 years (5 for Hong Kong holders).
Skilled visa income thresholds are indexed annually and change from 1 July each year.
The Employer Nomination Scheme (subclass 186) is a permanent visa with Direct Entry, Temporary Residence Transition and Labour Agreement streams.

The 186 Direct Entry stream generally requires being under 45 (unless exempt), a CSOL occupation, ≥3 years experience unless exempt, a positive skills assessment unless exempt, and English, health and character requirements, with permanent stay.
Subclass 482 holders may, if eligible, apply for permanent residence, including via employer sponsorship under the subclass 186 scheme.
The Skilled Independent visa (subclass 189) Points-tested stream is permanent, requiring a listed occupation, skills assessment, invitation and a passing points test.
The Skilled Nominated visa (subclass 190) is permanent, requiring state/territory nomination, a listed occupation, skills assessment, invitation and a passing points test.
A Designated Area Migration Agreement is a formal agreement between the Australian Government and a state/territory or regional authority giving access to more occupations than the standard program.
A DAMA uses a two-tier framework (a five-year head agreement plus employer labour agreements with concessions) and uses the subclass 482, 494 and 186 programs.
MARA maintains the register of registered migration agents and regulates the migration advice profession.
The Department of Home Affairs advises checking who can lawfully help with a visa application before paying anyone.

faq.question: Do I need both a visa and AHPRA registration?
faq.answerMarkdown: Generally yes. A visa from Home Affairs gives you the right to live and work in Australia; AHPRA registration gives you the right to practise medicine. They're separate, and one doesn't replace the other.

faq.question: Which visa do most international doctors use?
faq.answerMarkdown: Many enter on an employer-sponsored visa such as the Skills in Demand visa (subclass 482). The right pathway depends on your circumstances, take advice from a registered migration agent.

faq.question: Is the visa English requirement the same as the AHPRA one?
faq.answerMarkdown: No. The visa English standard is set by Home Affairs (e.g. IELTS 5.0 overall for the 482, unless exempt); AHPRA sets its own, often higher, registration English requirements. You must meet both separately.

faq.question: What's the difference between the Core Skills and Specialist Skills streams?
faq.answerMarkdown: Both are temporary 482 streams (up to 4 years; 5 for Hong Kong holders). Core Skills needs a CSOL occupation and the CSIT salary (AUD 76,515 for 2025-26); Specialist Skills targets higher-paid roles meeting the SSIT (AUD 141,210).

faq.question: Can a temporary visa lead to permanent residence?
faq.answerMarkdown: It can. Subclass 482 holders may, if eligible, apply for permanent residence, including via employer sponsorship under the subclass 186 scheme. Eligibility depends on your stream, occupation and circumstances.

faq.question: What is a DAMA and is it relevant for regional roles?
faq.answerMarkdown: A Designated Area Migration Agreement can give access to more occupations and concessions than the standard program, using the 482, 494 and 186 programs. For doctors heading to regional/rural areas it may open extra options; details are region-specific.

faq.question: Do these figures and lists change?
faq.answerMarkdown: Yes. Income thresholds are indexed annually (from 1 July) and occupation lists and rules change regularly. Confirm current figures on the official Home Affairs website.

faq.question: Should I use a migration agent?
faq.answerMarkdown: This page is general information, not migration advice. For your situation, use a registered migration agent regulated by MARA. Before paying anyone, Home Affairs advises checking who can lawfully help with a visa application.
